history: Die Deutsche Wochenschau was the unified weekly newsreel of Nazi Germany, produced by Universum Film AG (UFA) in Berlin and released in cinemas from June 1940 until 22 March 1945 (the last theatrical edition, No. 755). It was created by consolidating the four previously competing German newsreels — Ufa-Tonwoche, Deulig-Tonwoche, Tobis-Wochenschau and the Fox Tönende Wochenschau — into a single propaganda instrument. Its predecessor, the Ufa-Tonwoche, had run as UFA's weekly newsreel from 1925 (with synchronized sound from 1930) until it was merged in 1940. Throughout, production was supervised and censored by the Reich Ministry of Public Enlightenment and Propaganda under Joseph Goebbels, who used the newsreels to shape wartime public opinion; these films are now a core part of the audiovisual record of the Nazi era, preserved primarily by the German Federal Archives (Bundesarchiv).
production_location: Produced and edited by Universum Film AG (UFA) at its main building on Krausenstraße in central Berlin, Germany (synchronization and finishing done there until November 1943). NOTE for newsreels: the recording/event LOCATION is the place depicted in each issue (e.g. the Eastern Front, Stalingrad, Normandy, North Africa, occupied Poland), NOT Berlin where it was edited. Use Berlin/UFA only for the "studio/production" field; use the depicted event location for the per-video location tags.
date_logic: recordingDate = the BROADCAST/RELEASE date of that numbered newsreel edition (each issue has a unique number and a documented theatrical release date, e.g. No. 751 = 1945-02-10, No. 753 = 1945-03-05, No. 754 = 1945-03-16). Look up the edition number (visible on the title card or in the filename) against Bundesarchiv / Internet Archive catalog dates and use that exact release date. Do NOT use the editing date or a generic year. If only the year is known, use the broadcast year; never invent a specific day. The depicted events occurred shortly before the broadcast date (typically days to ~2 weeks prior).

history: Betty Boop was created by animator Grim Natwick at the request of Max Fleischer and produced by Fleischer Studios for distribution by Paramount Pictures. She debuted on August 9, 1930 in "Dizzy Dishes," the sixth entry in the Talkartoon series, and was inspired by the flapper-era singing style of Helen Kane; Mae Questel became her definitive voice from 1931 to 1939. After becoming the studio's breakout star, the Talkartoons were rebranded as her own Betty Boop series in August 1932, totaling roughly 90 theatrical shorts through 1939, many featuring jazz legends like Cab Calloway, Don Redman, and Louis Armstrong. From 1934, enforcement of the Motion Picture Production (Hays) Code toned down her risqué Pre-Code flapper image into a more demure career girl, making the early 1930s shorts prized examples of Pre-Code American animation.
production_location: Fleischer Studios, 1600 Broadway (at 48th Street), Midtown Manhattan, New York City, USA
date_logic: Use each cartoon's original US theatrical release date as recordingDate. These shorts are theatrical animation, so the documented Paramount/Fleischer release date (available per title on Wikipedia and IMDb, e.g. Dizzy Dishes = 1930-08-09) is the correct production/recording date. If only the year is known, use that production year (YYYY-01-01) and note it is approximate. Do not invent specific days; default to the verified release date for that specific short.

series: Superman (Fleischer Studios / Famous Studios, 1941-1943) — 17 theatrical Technicolor cartoon shorts released by Paramount Pictures
history: The Superman theatrical cartoons are a series of 17 animated Technicolor shorts released by Paramount Pictures between 1941 and 1943. Fleischer Studios produced the first 9 (1941-1942) at their Miami, Florida studio; after Paramount took control and reorganized the operation as Famous Studios, the final 8 (1942-1943) were produced in New York City. Lavishly budgeted (the first short cost about $50,000, the rest roughly $30,000 each) and made using rotoscoping for realistic motion, they are considered among the finest animation of Hollywood's Golden Age; the first short, "Superman," earned a 1941 Academy Award nomination for Best Short Subject (Cartoons). Bud Collyer voiced Superman and Joan Alexander voiced Lois Lane in the Fleischer entries, and the cartoons introduced Superman's now-iconic ability to fly. All 17 shorts are in the public domain in the United States because National/DC Comics failed to renew the copyrights (the original 35mm masters and ancillary rights are held today by Warner Bros.).
production_location: Shorts 1-9 (1941-1942): Fleischer Studios, Miami, Florida, USA. Shorts 10-17 (1942-1943): Famous Studios (Paramount Pictures), New York City, New York, USA. Distributed by Paramount Pictures.
date_logic: For each cartoon, set recordingDate to its original theatrical release date (the public premiere through Paramount), which is the well-documented, citable date for these shorts — not a separate "filming" date. Use these exact dates: Superman 1941-09-26; The Mechanical Monsters 1941-11-28; Billion Dollar Limited 1942-01-09; The Arctic Giant 1942-02-27; The Bulleteers 1942-03-27; The Magnetic Telescope 1942-04-24; Electric Earthquake 1942-05-15; Volcano 1942-07-10; Terror on the Midway 1942-08-30; Japoteurs 1942-09-18; Showdown 1942-10-16; Eleventh Hour 1942-11-20; Destruction, Inc. 1942-12-25; The Mummy Strikes 1943-02-19; Jungle Drums 1943-03-26; The Underground World 1943-06-18; Secret Agent 1943-07-30. If only a year is acceptable for a given field, use the release year (1941, 1942, or 1943 as appropriate). Do NOT invent more precise dates than the release date.

A golden-age Superman animated short distributed by Paramount Pictures. The 1941-1943 Superman series was produced by Fleischer Studios (Miami, Florida) and its successor Famous Studios (New York), and is famous for its lavish, rotoscoped Technicolor animation — among the finest of the Golden Age of American animation. These were the cartoons that first gave Superman the power of flight, with Bud Collyer as Superman and Joan Alexander as Lois Lane. All entries are in the public domain in the United States.

This restoration was created with AI upscaling and color/detail enhancement for modern displays. Original cartoon released {year} by Paramount Pictures; public domain.

history: Felix the Cat is the world's first animated pop-culture superstar, debuting as "Master Tom" in the Paramount short "Feline Follies" on 9 November 1919 and renamed Felix by his third appearance. The cartoons were produced at the Pat Sullivan Studio in Manhattan, New York: producer Pat Sullivan (an Australian-born immigrant) held the rights and marketed the character relentlessly, while lead animator Otto Messmer (American, from New Jersey) is widely credited by animation historians as Felix's true creative force, drawing well over a hundred shorts. Felix was the most popular cartoon character in the world through the mid-1920s — predating Disney's Mickey Mouse (1928) — distributed successively by Paramount/Famous Players-Lasky (1919–1921), M.J. Winkler/Winkler Pictures (1922–1925), Educational Pictures (1925–1928) and First National/Copley Pictures (1928–1930). The series declined when Sullivan resisted the switch to sound; these silent originals are now in the public domain.
production_location: Pat Sullivan Studio (Pat Sullivan Cartoon Studios), 47 West 63rd Street, Manhattan, New York City, USA. Note: Pat Sullivan was Australian-born, but the cartoons were animated in New York, USA — the production location is New York, not Australia.
date_logic: Each Felix entry is a theatrical animated short, so set recordingDate to the original US theatrical release date of that specific cartoon (e.g., "Feline Follies" = 1919-11-09; "Felix in Hollywood" = 1923-07-15). Use the exact day/month/year from the Felix the Cat filmography where available; if only the year is documented, use January 1 of that year (or the documented month). Do NOT use the modern AI-restoration/upload date as the recording date — that belongs in the upload metadata, not recordingDate. The {year} placeholder in descriptions is this original release year.

history: Popeye the Sailor began as a theatrical cartoon series in 1933, when Fleischer Studios (founded by brothers Max and Dave Fleischer, distributed by Paramount Pictures) adapted the character E.C. Segar had created for King Features' "Thimble Theatre" comic strip on January 17, 1929. Fleischer Studios produced 109 Popeye shorts from 1933 to 1942, first at 1600 Broadway in New York City and, from late 1938, at a new studio in Miami, Florida. In 1941-42 Paramount took control and renamed the studio Famous Studios, which produced a further 122 Popeye shorts from 1942 to 1957 (14 black-and-white, then color from "Her Honor the Mare"), ending with "Spooky Swabs" (1957). The early voice of Popeye was William Costello, later and most famously Jack Mercer; Olive Oyl was voiced by Mae Questel.
production_location: Fleischer Studios, 1600 Broadway, New York City, USA (1933-1938); then Fleischer Studios, Miami, Florida, USA (late 1938-1942); then Famous Studios, Miami, Florida and later New York City, USA (1942-1957). All distributed by Paramount Pictures.
date_logic: recordingDate = the cartoon's original U.S. theatrical RELEASE date (these are theatrical shorts, not broadcasts). Use the exact per-short release date from the Wikipedia Fleischer/Famous filmographies or IMDb (e.g. the debut "Popeye the Sailor" = 1933-07-14; "I Yam What I Yam" = 1933-09-29; "Blow Me Down!" = 1933-10-27). If only the year is known, use January 1 of that release year as a fallback. Do NOT use the 1929 comic-strip creation date for the cartoons; reserve 1929 only for franchise/character context in the description text.

series: Casper the Friendly Ghost (Famous Studios / Paramount Cartoon Studios)
history: Casper the Friendly Ghost is a series of theatrical animated shorts produced by Famous Studios (Paramount Pictures' in-house animation division, the successor to Fleischer Studios; renamed Paramount Cartoon Studios in 1956) and distributed by Paramount Pictures. The character was created by Seymour Reit and Joe Oriolo and debuted in the Noveltoon "The Friendly Ghost," released November 16, 1945 and directed by Isadore Sparber. After two further Noveltoons ("There's Good Boos To-Night," 1948; "A Haunting We Will Go," 1949), Casper graduated to his own series, with Paramount releasing dedicated Casper shorts from 1950 through 1959 for a total of 55 theatrical cartoons, making him Paramount's most popular cartoon character after Popeye.
production_location: Famous Studios (Paramount Cartoon Studios), Times Square / Broadway district, Manhattan, New York City, New York, USA. The studio operated out of office space in the Times Square area of New York from 1943 to 1967; no single confirmed street address is documented in reliable sources, so use "New York City, USA" for the location field and "Famous Studios, New York" / "Times Square, Manhattan" as supporting tags.
date_logic: For each Casper cartoon, set recordingDate to the documented original THEATRICAL RELEASE DATE of that specific short (these are well-documented for all 55 Famous Studios/Paramount shorts, 1945-1959). This is a production/release date, not a broadcast date. Examples: "The Friendly Ghost" = 1945-11-16; "There's Good Boos To-Night" = 1948-04-23; "A Haunting We Will Go" = 1949-05-13; "Casper's Spree Under the Sea" = 1950-10-27; "Casper's Birthday Party" = 1959-07-31 (final short). If the exact day is uncertain for a given title, fall back to the release year. Do NOT use the YouTube upload date or the AI-restoration date as the recordingDate.

history: Soundies were three-minute American musical films produced from 1940 to 1947 for the Mills Novelty Company's coin-operated "Panoram" jukebox, a 16mm rear-projection machine that played eight shorts for a dime in bars, diners, nightclubs and military bases. Over 1,850 titles were made by a series of affiliated companies (Globe, Cinemasters, Minoco, RCM, Filmcraft and others) and copyrighted/distributed by the Soundies Distributing Corporation of America, which issued a fresh reel weekly. Filmed in Hollywood, New York City and Chicago, they showcased Big Band, jazz and swing stars such as Duke Ellington, Count Basie, Cab Calloway, Louis Jordan, Fats Waller and Louis Armstrong, and helped launch Nat King Cole, Doris Day, Liberace and Dorothy Dandridge. Widely regarded as the historic precursor of the modern music video, most surviving Soundies form a priceless archive of 1940s American popular music.
production_location: United States — primarily three production hubs: Hollywood, California (RCM Productions); New York City (Minoco Productions and the Mills/Fritz Pollard office, with numbers shot on a Bronx sound stage — the former Edison studio on Decatur Avenue — and rehearsed at Suntan Studios in Harlem); and Chicago, Illinois (Mills Novelty Company's own facility, est. 1942). Distributor: Soundies Distributing Corporation of America (subsidiary of Mills Novelty Company, Chicago).
date_logic: Use the official release date assigned by the Soundies Distributing Corporation of America as the recordingDate (Soundies were released on dated weekly reels, generally within a few weeks of filming, 1940-1947). If a precise per-title release date is unknown, fall back to the documented copyright registration date for that title, then to the filming year; never invent a day/month. For a title only known by year, use that year (e.g. 1942-01-01 as a year-level placeholder is acceptable only if the platform requires a full date — otherwise record just the year). Decade tag: 1940s.

history: Alfred J. Kwak is a children's animated comedy-drama series based on a stage show by Dutch singer-songwriter Herman van Veen, who created the duckling character and wrote the episode synopses. The 52-episode series was produced by Dennis Livson's Telecable Benelux B.V. as an international co-production with Dutch broadcaster VARA, Germany's ZDF, Spain's Televisión Española and Japan's TV Tokyo, with animation handled by Telescreen Japan in Tokyo. Character and production design came from German artists Harald Siepermann and Hans Bacher, while Japanese staff including director Hiroshi Saitō and writer Akira Miyazaki executed the animation. It premiered on TV Tokyo on 3 April 1989 and later reached the Netherlands (1989), Germany (ZDF, 1990) and Spain (1991), and is remembered for tackling mature themes like discrimination, dictatorship and environmentalism.
production_location: Animation produced at Telescreen Japan in Tokyo, Japan; an international co-production led by Telecable Benelux B.V. (Netherlands) with ZDF (Germany), Televisión Española (Spain) and TV Tokyo (Japan). Concept originated in the Netherlands (Herman van Veen); design work by German artists Harald Siepermann and Hans Bacher.
date_logic: This is a copyrighted 1989 series, not public domain. Per episode, use the ORIGINAL first-broadcast date as recordingDate. The earliest reference is the TV Tokyo Japanese premiere run (3 April 1989 to 29 March 1990). If episode-level Japanese air dates are unknown, fall back to the series production year 1989 (use 1989-01-01 as a safe placeholder, or the country-specific original air date if cataloguing a specific national dub: Netherlands/VARA from 24 Dec 1989, Germany/ZDF from 13 Oct 1990, Spain/TVE from 20 Apr 1991). Never invent a precise day you cannot source; default to the production year 1989 when uncertain.

series: Classic Public-Domain Films & Silent Horror (German Expressionism + early Hollywood horror)
history: This series collects landmark public-domain feature films of the silent and early-sound era, centered on German Expressionist horror and the first American horror talkies. Das Cabinet des Dr. Caligari (1920, Decla-Bioscop, Berlin) launched Expressionist cinema with its painted, distorted sets; F.W. Murnau's Nosferatu (1922, Prana-Film) gave the screen its first vampire and the iconic Count Orlok; Fritz Lang's Metropolis (1927, UFA Babelsberg) became the most expensive and influential silent science-fiction epic; and White Zombie (1932, Halperin Productions / United Artists, Los Angeles) introduced the word "zombie" to cinema with Bela Lugosi. These restorations present works that shaped the visual language of horror and sci-fi worldwide. Note on rights: most are public domain in the US; Metropolis is PD in the US (since 1 Jan 2023) but remains under copyright in the EU until 2046 (Lang died 1976), and Nosferatu's status differs by country — verify per title before publishing.
production_location: Per title: Das Cabinet des Dr. Caligari — Decla-Bioscop (Lixie-Atelier), Berlin-Weißensee, Germany. Nosferatu — Prana-Film; studio work in Berlin, location shooting in Wismar and Lübeck (Germany) and the High Tatras/Slovakia. Metropolis — Universum Film AG (UFA), Babelsberg Studios, Potsdam/Berlin, Germany. White Zombie — Halperin Productions, filmed on the Universal Studios lot and Bronson Canyon, Los Angeles, California, USA (released by United Artists). Note: YouTube location cannot be set via API (Studio-only); use these place names in description text and tags instead.
date_logic: For these feature films, recordingDate should be the film's ORIGINAL theatrical premiere/release date (production year), NOT the AI-restoration date. Use the earliest verifiable public premiere: Caligari = 1920-02-26 (Marmorhaus, Berlin); Nosferatu = 1922-03-15 (cinema premiere, Primus-Palast Berlin; the 1922-03-04 gala was an invite-only preview); Metropolis = 1927-01-10 (Ufa-Palast am Zoo, Berlin); White Zombie = 1932-07-28 (US release). If only the year is reliably known for a given title, use January 1 of that year as a documented fallback and state the year in the description. Always cite the source year; never invent a precise day.

series: Der kleine Maulwurf / Krtek (The Little Mole)
history: Der kleine Maulwurf (Czech: Krtek/Krteček, "The Little Mole") is an animated series created by Czech animator and illustrator Zdeněk Miler (1921–2011). The first film, "Jak krtek ke kalhotkám přišel" (How the Mole Got His Trousers), was produced in 1957 at the Bratři v triku studio of Krátký film Praha in Prague, Czechoslovakia, and won a directing award at the Venice Film Festival. Roughly 49–50 short films were made between 1957 and 2002; only the first contains narration, after which Miler used his daughters' wordless exclamations so the cartoons could be understood worldwide. NOTE: Krtek is NOT public domain — it remains under copyright held by Miler's estate (a well-documented heirs' dispute followed his 2011 death), so it should not be labeled as a public-domain work.
production_location: Bratři v triku studio, Krátký film Praha (Prague Short Film studio), Prague, Czechoslovakia (today Czech Republic). Creator Zdeněk Miler was born in Kladno, near Prague.
date_logic: These are produced animated shorts, not broadcasts, so recordingDate = the production/release year of each individual episode (use YYYY-01-01 when only the year is known). Examples: "Jak krtek ke kalhotkám přišel" = 1957-01-01; "Krtek a autíčko" (The Mole and the Car) = 1963-01-01; "Krtek a raketa" (The Mole and the Rocket) = 1965-01-01. Do NOT use a single series-wide date and do NOT use a modern upload/restoration date as the recordingDate — match each video to its original episode year. The 8K AI restoration year is metadata for the description (e.g., "AI-restored in 8K, 2026"), not the recordingDate.

description_template:
{title} ({year}) — Der kleine Maulwurf / Krtek | The Little Mole

Ein klassischer tschechischer Zeichentrickfilm von Zdeněk Miler, ursprünglich {year} im Studio Krátký film Praha (Bratři v triku) in Prag produziert. Mit KI in atemberaubender 8K-Qualität restauriert. Der kleine Maulwurf kommt fast ohne Worte aus und ist deshalb in der ganzen Welt verständlich.

A classic Czech cartoon by Zdeněk Miler, originally produced in {year} at the Krátký film Praha studio (Bratři v triku) in Prague. AI-restored in stunning 8K. Krtek the Little Mole is almost wordless and beloved worldwide.

🌍 Auch bekannt als / Also known as: The Little Mole (EN) · Krtek / Krteček (CZ) · La Petite Taupe (FR) · El topo / Otto el topo (ES/Latinoamérica) · クルテク / もぐらくん (JA) · Krecik (PL) · 두더지 Krtek (KO)
